questions.question: Hoke observed the principle of MT by accident around 1918. What did de Forest and Doane contribute in the 1930s that made it an industrial method?
options: They discovered that compass needles deflect over flaws
Deliberate magnetization in known directions, two perpendicular fields for coverage, and engineered particles
The first portable radiographic camera for field welds
The replacement of magnetic particles with penetrating dyes
questions.explanation: De Forest and Doane turned the accidental observation into a controlled method: known field directions, two roughly perpendicular magnetizations, and purpose-made particles - then founded a company in 1934 to supply it.

questions.question: What advance in the early 1940s gave magnetic particle testing a major step up in sensitivity?
options: Half-wave rectified current
Fluorescent particles that glow yellow-green under ultraviolet light
The magnetic chuck
Digital recording of indications
questions.explanation: Fluorescent particles, introduced in the early 1940s, glow brightly under UV light in a darkened area, multiplying contrast between indication and background compared with visible particles.

questions.question: A forging shop can catch a seam at the rough-machining stage or risk finding it after finish machining, plating, and assembly. Why does NDT applied early reduce cost?
options: Early testing is exempt from documentation requirements
Flaws found early void the material supplier's warranty
Each later manufacturing stage adds value to the part, so scrapping it later wastes more invested cost - and an escape to service can cost far more
NDT equipment is cheaper to operate on rough surfaces
questions.explanation: The economic case for NDT rests on early rejection: a part scrapped at the billet stage has little invested cost, while the same flaw found after finishing - or in service - wastes all downstream value and can cause a failure.

questions.question: Which pairing of NDT method and typical strength is correct?
options: Liquid penetrant testing - subsurface flaws in steel plate
Eddy current testing - flaws and properties in conductive tubing and aircraft structure
Radiographic testing - only surface-breaking cracks
Ultrasonic testing - examination of nonconductive coatings only
questions.explanation: ET induces currents in any conductive material and excels on tubing, aircraft structure, conductivity sorting, and coatings. PT is surface-breaking only, RT images internal volumetric flaws, and UT finds internal flaws and measures thickness.

questions.question: In personnel qualification language, 'certification' means:
options: Completing the required classroom training hours
Written testimony by the employer or a central body that an individual is qualified
Passing the practical examination alone
Accumulating supervised on-the-job experience
questions.explanation: Training and experience contribute to qualification; certification is the written testimony of that qualification, issued by the employer under SNT-TC-1A or by a central body under schemes like ISO 9712.

questions.question: A Level I MT technician is asked to run routine bench tests. Which statement correctly bounds that work?
options: The Level I may perform the tests per written instructions and record results, but not independently interpret or evaluate them
The Level I may interpret and evaluate, but not record results
The Level I may modify the procedure if production demands it
The Level I may train and examine other technicians
questions.explanation: Level I scope is performing specific calibrations and tests under written instructions and recording results; independent interpretation and evaluation begin at Level II, and procedure authority belongs to Level III.

questions.question: Which vision requirement is typical for certified MT personnel?
options: Distance vision checked once at initial certification only
Near-vision acuity verified periodically - typically annually - plus verified color/contrast perception
Peripheral vision mapping every five years
No vision requirements apply to MT because indications are large
questions.explanation: Reading fine particle indications is close visual work, so near-vision acuity is verified on a typical annual cycle, and adequate color or contrast perception is verified as well - lapsed exams can invalidate certifications.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly orders the core steps of a basic magnetic particle test?
options: Apply particles, magnetize, clean the surface, interpret, document
Clean the surface, verify system and lighting, magnetize and apply particles, interpret and evaluate, repeat perpendicular, record and demagnetize
Demagnetize, apply particles, magnetize, clean the surface, report
Magnetize in one direction only, apply particles, post-clean, report
questions.explanation: The backbone is preparation, system verification, magnetization with particle application (continuous method), interpretation and evaluation, a second roughly perpendicular magnetization, then records, demagnetization as required, and cleanup.

questions.question: Compared with liquid penetrant testing on the same steel weld, magnetic particle testing offers which genuine advantage?
options: It works equally well on austenitic stainless filler metal
It can reveal discontinuities slightly below the surface and tolerates less stringent surface preparation
It finds deep internal porosity throughout the weld volume
It never requires two field directions
questions.explanation: MT detects near-surface discontinuities that PT cannot (PT needs a surface opening) and is more tolerant of surface condition - but it is still limited to ferromagnetic material and still needs two perpendicular magnetizations.

questions.question: A hardened, high-carbon steel part holds a strong field after the current stops; a soft low-carbon bracket does not. Which conclusion is correct?
options: The bracket has higher retentivity and suits the residual method
The hardened part has high retentivity, making it a candidate for the residual method; the bracket should be tested by the continuous method
Both parts are equally suited to the residual method
Retentivity is a property of the particles, not the part
questions.explanation: Retentivity - the ability to retain a residual field - is high in hard, high-carbon steels and low in soft steels. Only high-retentivity parts qualify for the residual method; the continuous method works for both and is the default.
